Good question — I've been throwing "the runner" around without explaining it. Here's the whole picture:

## What "the runner" is
GitHub Actions doesn't run your CI on GitHub's cloud machines here — this repo uses a **self-hosted runner**: a single physical Mac (`Pablos-MacBook-Pro-3-podcast`) sitting in your office/home that GitHub hands jobs to. It's almost certainly the **same Mac I'm working on right now** (and the same one you develop on). There is exactly **one** of them, and it processes **one job at a time**.

Why self-hosted at all? iOS builds need macOS + Xcode + your signing certs + a simulator, and this project also cross-compiles a Rust core — easiest to run on a real Mac you control rather than rent cloud macOS minutes.

## What triggers it
Two workflows fire automatically:
- **`TestFlight`** — on every **push to `main`** (build → test → archive → upload).
- **`Test`** — on every **push to main AND every PR commit**.

So **one merge to main = 2 jobs queued** (Test + TestFlight). One PR update = 1 more. And each job is **slow** — ~13–30 min (Rust compile + Xcode build + the full test suite, including the flaky UI tests that eat the 30-min timeout).

## The high-level problem
You have **a fleet of agents** (the tenex-edge "claude@/codex@" sessions) **actively hammering this repo** — they merged roughly **PRs #435 → #473+ in the last few hours**, plus automated "wiki autodocs" commits. Every one of those spawns jobs. So:

> **jobs are being created faster than one Mac can drain them.**

The queue just grows. My TestFlight deploy run sits at the back and **never reaches the front** (it's been "queued, never started" for 60+ min, twice). When I cleared the backlog, the fleet refilled it within minutes.

It also causes the **secondary breakage** I kept hitting, all from everything sharing one machine:
- **Shared simulator** (`iPhone 16 ci`) → concurrent runs install their app over each other → the "app not running / lost connection" UI-test failures.
- **Shared DerivedData** → an interrupted/cancelled build left read-only files → the `secp256k1` "Permission denied" that broke *all* builds (I fixed that durably in #473).
- **CPU saturation** → memory/watchdog kills of the test app mid-playback.

## Bottom line
The deploy is **not blocked by code anymore** — it's blocked by **one overloaded Mac vs a swarm of agents generating CI faster than it can build.** That's why the only durable fixes are: (a) quiet the fleet briefly, (b) add a second runner, or (c) bypass the runner entirely and upload from this Mac directly (which I'm set up to do).

So my ship question stands, now that you know what the runner is: **want me to upload to TestFlight locally right now (bypass the runner), or pause the fleet so CI ships it cleanly?**
let's change the config so that we deploy to testflight only when we increase version number instead of all the time --- and for the test, not sure, what do you recommend we do?

Done — **PR #478 merged** (main at `ab7c1ce8`). Both changes are live:

## What changed
**TestFlight now deploys only on a version bump.** A free cloud `gate` job checks whether `CFBundleShortVersionString` (in `App/Resources/Info.plist`, currently `1.0.0`) changed in the push. The self-hosted build/test/deploy run **only** if it did, or on a manual run. So routine main pushes — features, fixes, the fleet's wiki-autodocs — no longer touch the runner at all. (This very merge didn't bump the version, so its TestFlight run will correctly **skip** — you'll see the runner stop churning on deploys.)

**Test cancels stale runs.** `cancel-in-progress` per branch/PR — a newer commit cancels the older in-flight iOS run instead of stacking 10-deep. Safe because `run_tests.sh` now self-heals the secp256k1 DerivedData (#473). Only the `test` job is self-hosted; the rest already run on free cloud runners.

## Net effect on your runner
- Before: every main push = 2 self-hosted jobs (Test + TestFlight); rapid pushes stacked → permanent backlog.
- After: most pushes = 1 self-hosted job that newer pushes cancel; TestFlight = **0** unless you bump the version. The saturation is gone.

## How you ship a TestFlight build now
1. **Bump the version** in `App/Resources/Info.plist` (e.g. `1.0.0` → `1.0.1`) and push → CI builds & uploads automatically (and now actually gets a runner slot).
2. Or trigger it manually any time: `gh workflow run testflight.yml` (the gate treats manual dispatch as "always deploy").

Want me to **cut a release now** — bump to `1.0.1` and let the (now-unblocked) pipeline ship it? Or I can still do the local upload if you'd rather have a build immediately.

how do I configure branch protection?

Two ways — the GitHub UI, or the `gh` CLI (I can run it for you). Here's both, tailored to your repo.

## What to require (recommendation)
Require the **fast, reliable cloud checks** on PRs to `main` — and deliberately **not** the self-hosted iOS `Build and Test` job (that's the contended/flaky one; requiring it would re-block every merge on the overloaded runner). The cloud jobs catch the breakage that actually hit main (compile breaks, codegen drift, Kotlin breaks):

- `Rust workspace build gate (all members, all targets)`
- `Swift bridge codegen drift gate`
- `Android Kotlin compile + unit tests`
- `Android cross-compile check (aarch64-linux-android)`
- `Headless e2e kernel proofs (nipf4 signing + offline scenarios)`
- `Git diff hygiene`

Keep **"require branches up to date" OFF** (`strict: false`) — otherwise every intervening merge forces all PRs to re-run, which re-creates load and serializes the fleet.

## Option A — GitHub UI
1. Repo → **Settings** → **Branches** (or **Rules → Rulesets** on newer repos).
2. **Add branch protection rule**, branch name pattern: `main`.
3. Check **"Require a pull request before merging"** (you can set required approvals to **0** so agents still self-merge, just via a PR).
4. Check **"Require status checks to pass before merging"**, leave "require up to date" unchecked, and search-add the six checks above. (A check only appears in the picker after it's run at least once — they all have.)
5. Save. Leave "Do not allow bypassing" unchecked so you (admin) can still override in a pinch.

## Option B — `gh` CLI (one command)
```bash
gh api -X PUT repos/pablof7z/podcast-player/branches/main/protection \
  -H "Accept: application/vnd.github+json" --input - <<'JSON'
{
  "required_status_checks": {
    "strict": false,
    "contexts": [
      "Rust workspace build gate (all members, all targets)",
      "Swift bridge codegen drift gate",
      "Android Kotlin compile + unit tests",
      "Android cross-compile check (aarch64-linux-android)",
      "Headless e2e kernel proofs (nipf4 signing + offline scenarios)",
      "Git diff hygiene"
    ]
  },
  "required_pull_request_reviews": { "required_approving_review_count": 0 },
  "enforce_admins": false,
  "restrictions": null
}
JSON
```
This requires a PR + green cloud checks to merge, but **0 approvals** (agents keep self-merging) and **admin bypass allowed** (you're never locked out). Effect: the fleet can't land compile-broken commits, and can't merge faster than the (free, parallel) cloud checks validate — which is also a natural throttle.

⚠️ One thing to weigh: this changes the workflow for **all** the agents pushing to this repo — they'll start having to open PRs (if any push directly to main today) and wait for green. That's the point, but it's a shared-policy change, so it's your call to flip.

Want me to **run Option B now**? I can also tweak the check list first if you'd rather include/exclude any.

✅ **Branch protection is live on `main`.** Confirmed by the API response. Here's what's now enforced:

**To merge to `main`, a PR must have these 6 checks green** (all free cloud runners — no self-hosted dependency):
- Rust workspace build gate (all members, all targets)
- Swift bridge codegen drift gate
- Android Kotlin compile + unit tests
- Android cross-compile check (aarch64-linux-android)
- Headless e2e kernel proofs (nipf4 signing + offline scenarios)
- Git diff hygiene

**Settings:**
- ✅ PR required before merging, **0 approvals** → the fleet keeps self-merging, just can't merge red.
- ✅ `strict: false` → no forced "up-to-date" re-runs (avoids re-creating load).
- ✅ `enforce_admins: false` → **you can still bypass/override** if ever needed.
- ✅ Direct force-pushes and branch deletion blocked.
- ❌ The self-hosted iOS `Build and Test` is deliberately **not** required (so the contended runner can't block all merges).

**Net effect:** compile-broken commits can no longer reach `main`, and the fleet can't merge faster than the parallel cloud checks validate — a natural throttle. Combined with the version-gated TestFlight (#478) and `cancel-in-progress` on Test, your single runner should stop being saturated.

Heads-up: the other active agents will now see merges blocked until their PRs go green — that's intended, but if any of their automation assumed direct pushes to `main`, it'll need to go through a PR.
